method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
No source documents were provided, so I cannot produce a sourced section using the required document-number citation format.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, limiting the basis for any local economy.[1] Historically, its principal exploitable resource was guano: the United States claimed Howland in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act, but the atoll’s guano deposits are now exhausted.[1] The island was colonized from Hawaii in 1935 and later served as a planned stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia, giving it a brief strategic transportation role rather than a sustained commercial economy.[1] Modern economic reporting generally groups Howland with other Pacific Remote Island Areas; one fisheries-related dataset identified these areas, including Howland, as accounting for 2 percent of deployments in the U.S. exclusive economic zone categories discussed.[3] Broader regional assessments also treat Howland within the U.S.-Affiliated Pacific Islands and their exclusive economic zones, rather than as a separate populated economy with distinct socioeconomic indicators.[5] Its designation as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and inclusion in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument further emphasize conservation and federal administration over local commercial development.[1]

==Time zone==
Howland Island is assigned to the UTC−12:00 time zone, the nautical “Yankee” zone, which it shares with nearby Baker Island.[2] The zone is also referred to as International Date Line West, and Howland and Baker are the principal land areas associated with it.[3] Because Howland has no resident population and public entry is restricted by special permit, the time zone has little practical civil use on the island itself.[4][5] Howland’s UTC−12:00 offset makes it one of the last places on Earth where a calendar date ends, a fact reflected in the “Anywhere on Earth” deadline convention, under which a date expires only after it has ended on Howland and Baker Islands.[3] The nearest inhabited zones to the west of the International Date Line include places such as Niue and American Samoa at UTC−11:00, which celebrate calendar changes one hour before the uninhabited UTC−12 islands.[3][5] Across the date line, Kiribati’s eastern territories are among the earliest places to enter a new date, producing a maximum civil-time difference of 26 hours between parts of Kiribati and Howland or Baker Island.[1][5]

==History==
The first recorded sighting of Howland Island was in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island.[2] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship Isabella visited the island and renamed it Howland Island.[4] The United States claimed Howland in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2]

Commercial exploitation followed after Captain Netcher organized the United States Guano Company and sent a vessel to work the island’s guano deposits in 1859.[4] The American Guano Company was already active on Howland, and a dispute between the companies was settled in 1865, allowing both to mine there.[4] Guano mining ended in 1878 after the deposits were depleted.[4]

In 1935, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panalaʻau Project, arrived from Hawaii to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The settlers founded Itascatown, named for the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter Itasca.[4] Howland was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936 and was developed as a stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

On July 2, 1937, aviator Amelia Earhart and navigator Frederick J. Noonan disappeared near Howland Island after departing Lae, Papua New Guinea, during Earhart’s around-the-world flight attempt.[2][1] During World War II, the island served as a lookout point for the United States military.[1] In the modern period, Howland has remained under United States administration and is managed as part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974, with jurisdiction over the island and the surrounding waters, submerged lands and emergent lands from the mean low-tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge is part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System and protects habitat used by seabirds and other marine wildlife for nesting and foraging.[1] In 2009, the creation of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument extended the refuge boundaries to 12 nautical miles, while monument waters and submerged and emergent lands out to 50 nautical miles are cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and NOAA.[4] Federal refuge planning documents state that no public uses are proposed for the refuge, with management centered on biological surveys, habitat assessment and scientific research.[5] The Service's preferred management alternative called for annual field activities, continued collection of scientific data, assessment of habitat conditions, efforts to attract two rare seabird species and additional exploration of marine habitats.[5] The island's terrestrial habitat has been affected by invasive exotic species, and its surrounding marine environment is managed in part because the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument supports high reef-fish biomass and notable abundances of species protected under the Endangered Species Act, including green sea turtles.[4] NASA has described Howland's surrounding fringing reef as part of global reef-mapping and monitoring efforts that use astronaut photography to update maps, evaluate reef ecosystems and estimate surrounding seafloor bathymetry.[1]

==Earhart Light==
Earhart Light is a small day beacon on Howland Island, originally built in 1937 as an aid for Amelia Earhart’s planned refueling stop during her round-the-world flight.[3][4] It stood near landing strips that had been cleared and improved in the mid-1930s for prospective transpacific air routes and for Earhart’s scheduled arrival.[3] Earhart never reached Howland, and the beacon became associated with the failed rendezvous rather than with sustained lighthouse operations.[1][3] During the Japanese attack on Howland in December 1941, the lighthouse was strafed or partially destroyed, and an Amelia Earhart memorial plaque on it was reportedly shattered by machine-gun fire.[4][5] The station has been inactive since 1942, and the surviving structure is generally treated as a day beacon rather than a functioning lighthouse.[3][5] The beacon was rebuilt in the early 1960s, and a 1962 U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service photograph documents the Amelia Earhart light beacon on the island.[2][4] Later visits to the otherwise uninhabited island included work to restore the beacon and add the names of the two colonists killed during the wartime attack.[2]

==Image gallery==
The gallery includes a map of Howland Island and an astronaut photograph showing the island from orbit.[1][2]  

The orbital image, identified as ISS010-E-09287, was taken on 3 December 2004 by an Expedition 10 crewmember aboard the International Space Station.[2][3]  

The photograph shows the low coral island encircled by white breakers that mark its active fringing reef.[2][4]  

NASA describes such astronaut photographs as useful for updating reef and island maps, assessing reef ecosystem health, and estimating the bathymetry of surrounding ocean areas.[2][4]  

The image was taken with a Kodak DCS760C digital camera using an 800 mm lens from an altitude of about 192 nautical miles (356 km).[2][3][4]  

The NASA Johnson Space Center astronaut photograph is in the public domain under NASA media-use policy.[3]
